planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, sandy, infertile, acidic soil in full sun; sandhill milkweed is native to the dry, sandy pine flatwoods and sandhills of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, where it grows in the challenging, xeric conditions typical of the longleaf pine ecosystem.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, infertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; sandhill milkweed is a prostrate to spreading Gulf Coastal Plain milkweed adapted to the extreme xeric conditions of longleaf pine sandhills — the type of habitat that receives very little soil moisture even during rainy seasons due to the high permeability of deep, white sand. The plant lies almost flat along the ground, with large, round, grayish-green leaves that help reduce water loss, and produces clusters of pale pink to lavender flowers in summer. It is a primary monarch butterfly host plant in the longleaf pine flatwoods ecosystem and an important nectar source for numerous native bees. One of the most drought-tolerant milkweeds available, making it an outstanding pollinator plant for dry, sunny, sandy landscapes in zone 9a.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; red milkweed grows naturally in freshwater and brackish marshes, wet pine flatwoods, and tidal ditches of the Coastal Plain. Tolerates seasonal flooding but not prolonged submersion.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun at pond margins, rain gardens, and tidal wetland edges; red milkweed is one of the most brilliantly colored of all native milkweeds, producing vivid, bicolored flowers with red-orange reflexed petals and orange hoods — the most intensely colored flower heads in the Asclepias genus. It grows naturally in freshwater and brackish marshes and wet pine flatwoods of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, making it ideally suited to rain gardens and wet bioswales in zone 9a. The striking flowers attract monarchs, swallowtails, and a wide range of other pollinators, and the plant is an important monarch host plant in Gulf Coast wetlands. The tall, narrow habit with narrow, willow-like leaves gives it a graceful, architectural form in the moist garden.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; clasping milkweed grows in dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, open habitats — prairies, savannas, and open pine woodlands. It is named for its broad leaves that clasp the stem at the base.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun in prairie, savanna, or open woodland garden settings; clasping milkweed is a striking native with unusually large, broad, blue-green leaves that wrap around the stem — the 'clasping' or 'amplexicaul' leaf base is the most distinctive feature. The rounded, rosy-pink to purple-pink flower heads in late spring to early summer are produced at the stem tips and have a subtle, sweet fragrance. The species is common in dry, sandy grasslands and open pine woodlands across the eastern US and is an important monarch caterpillar host plant. Clasping milkweed develops a deep taproot that makes it difficult to transplant — direct sow or plant very young plants without disturbing the root. An outstanding native for dry, open prairie gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; poke milkweed is uniquely adapted among milkweeds for shaded conditions, growing naturally in moist, mesic deciduous forests and woodland edges in the eastern US.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; poke milkweed is a critically important species for expanding milkweed diversity in the landscape — it is one of the only milkweeds adapted to partial shade conditions, naturally growing at the forest edge, in woodland openings, and along shaded roadsides in the Appalachians and surrounding regions. Most milkweeds require full sun and would not survive the partial shade gardens where many gardeners could offer milkweed habitat. Poke milkweed fills this niche, producing tall stems with nodding, drooping clusters of white to pale pink flowers with purple hoods that are attractive to monarch butterflies as both larval host and adult nectar source. A tall, imposing plant that fits naturally at the woodland edge or in the back of a shade border.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; green milkweed grows in dry, open prairies, glades, and rocky slopes across the eastern and central US.

directions: Plant in dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; green milkweed is the only entirely green-flowered milkweed species in the eastern flora, producing small, compact clusters of unusual lime-green flowers that lack the contrasting hoods of most milkweeds. The subdued coloration makes it a plant that rewards close inspection rather than impressing from a distance. Despite the unshowy flowers, it is a fully functional monarch host plant and provides the same monarch caterpillar habitat value as any other milkweed species. It is adapted to xeric, rocky prairie conditions with very low moisture and nutrient levels. An underappreciated native milkweed for dry, sunny, lean-soil gardens where it completes the spectrum of native milkweed diversity. Specialist bee species that collect milkweed pollen pollinate the flowers.
